    ColorsMidnight, Starlight, Green, Blue, Red, Graphite, Gold, Silver, Space Black, TitaniumMidnight, Starlight, Silver, Pink, Red, Graphite, Gold
    CPUApple S7Apple S9 SiP
    Storage32GB64GB
    Health sensorsBlood oxygen sensor, electrical heart sensor, third-generation optical heart sensor, accelerometer, gyroscopeBlood oxygen sensor, electrical heart sensor, third-generation optical heart sensor, temperature sensor, high-g accelerometer, high dynamic range gyroscope
    Price$399$399
    Weight32g (41mm), 38.8g (45mm)31.9g (41mm); 38.7g (45mm)
    Brightness1,000 nits2,000 nits
    Water resistance50 meters50 meters

Design, build, and display

When buying a new device, you need to consider, first off, any outward differences. In this case, however, there's not much to compare, as both the Apple Watch Series 9 and Series 7 share identical elements. For starters, they both look the same on the outside, and you pretty much can't tell the two apart. You get to pick between 41mm and 45mm sizes in both cases, and you also get to choose between an aluminum or stainless steel build. Unfortunately, the Series 7 exclusively offers a titanium build and a wider variety of colors, as highlighted in the previous section.

Otherwise, both watches can resist up to 50 meters of water and have curved screens that support the always-on display feature. Speaking of the display, though, the Apple Watch Series 9 has a 2,000-nit peak brightness, double what the Series 7 offers. So, if you tend to use your watch in direct sunlight often, then you may appreciate the Series 9's brighter screen. Everything else about the screen is the same, though.

Performance and features

Apple Watch series 9. 

The gap between the Apple Watch Series 9 and Series 7 is larger in the performance department. For starters, Apple has finally boosted the CPU for the first time in three years with the S9 SiP. This upgrade is accompanied by double the storage (64GB vs. 32GB), faster and offline Siri support, a double-tap pinch gesture to interact with watchOS 10, precision iPhone finding, and better HomePod integration. That's not to mention that the Series 9 also packs perks introduced with the Series 8, such as crash detection, menstrual cycle tracking through a temperature sensor, and more.

However, while the aforementioned sensors and features are nice to have, they likely won't impact how you interact with your watch day-to-day. After all, both watches offer the same battery life, fast charging, and fitness and health tracking, such as ECG, heart rate measurements, blood oxygen monitoring, calories burnt, and more. And they're rated for similar battery life — up to 18 hours or 36 in Low Power Mode. Plus, the Series 7 will also get an upgrade to watchOS 10, so it'll get the subsequent UI overhauls. Ultimately, both the Series 7 and Series 9 will offer similar experiences, but the latter model packs a few extra perks.

Apple Watch Series 9 vs Series 7: Which smartwatch is better?

The Apple Watch Series 9 in pink

The Apple Watch Series 9 is undoubtedly the winner of this battle. It offers the exact same specs and features offered by the Series 7 and more. That's excluding the titanium build option and the wider variety of colors — which aren't as much of a big deal anyway. So, if you don't already have a Series 7 or Series 8, then the Series 9 is definitely an excellent pick for $399. If you already have a Series 7, however, then you may want to hold on to it, as the upgrades included with the Series 9 arguably aren't worth the cost.

Apple Watch Series 9
Editor's choice

Apple's Watch Series 9 packs in a new, more powerful processor, a brighter display capable of up to 2,000 nits peak brightness, Apple's U2 chip that enables precision-finding of your iPhone, as well as better HomePod integration, and a whole slew of features and tech you'd expect.

$390 at Amazon $400 at Best Buy $399 at Apple

If you're upgrading from a Series 6 or a non-watchOS wearable, then the Series 7 is an acceptable pick if you find it heavily (50% off) discounted. You may still come across some major deals, as some retailers haven't gotten rid of their old stock yet. Otherwise, do not buy an Apple Watch Series 7 at a full $399 price. The Series 9 matches the price tag while offering more perks and technology.

Apple Watch Series 7
Acceptable alternative
$472 $749 Save $277

The Apple Watch Series 7 has been discontinued and can cost as much as the Series 9. Only buy it if it's notably discounted, as it offers a similar set of main features and health sensors.

$472 at Amazon $749 at Walmart

item.title: How to update iOS and iPadOS with your Mac
item.itunes:title: How to update iOS and iPadOS with your Mac
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Software updates can have a significant impact on your device, from adding vibrant new features to bringing critical security fixes. While older iPhone, iPod, and iPad software updates required a computer to be installed, many updates can now be applied over the air. That means all you need is your device to start the process automatically. However, it can be useful to install an update on another device. In some cases, it may be absolutely necessary. Luckily, you can use a Mac computer or laptop to update your iPhone or iPad to the latest version of iOS 17 or iPadOS 17.

Why you might need to use a Mac to update iOS

iOS 17's app library

Software updates, especially yearly upgrades, can come with extremely large file sizes. For example, the upgrade to iOS 17 has a file size of between 5 and 6GB. If you're using a base-model device with 64GB or 128GB of storage, you simply might not have the space to download the upgrade file if you have a lot of apps or data. However, since upgrade files are downloaded and subsequently deleted after the update is applied, it's not necessary to have them on-device.

To get around storage issues, you can download the update to your Mac — which probably has more available storage space — and install it to your iOS or iPadOS device via a cable. This might also be preferred if your Mac has an Ethernet connection, as you can download the update quicker than over a wireless network.

What you need to know before getting started

There are a few qualifications to updating your iOS or iPadOS device with a Mac. The computer in question must be running macOS Catalina or later, which was released in October 2019. Older Macs with earlier versions of macOS or Mac OS X may be able to update your device, but only in iTunes.

Before you update, you should back up your iOS or iPadOS device because there is always a risk of data loss when applying updates. Finally, make sure that your computer isn't using your phone's personal hotspot for a connection, as that'll will be terminated when your device restarts.

Connecting your iOS or iPadOS device to your Mac

Before you can install an update to your iOS or iPadOS device via your Mac, you need to get the devices connected. To do so, follow the steps below.

  1. Connect your iOS or iPadOS device to your Mac via a cable. The cable must be capable of data transfer.
  2. Follow the on-screen prompts on your Mac to allow your iPhone to connect. Click the Trust or Allow buttons as they appear.
  3. Follow the same steps on your iOS or iPadOS device. Press the Trust button as it appears, and enter your password or passcode.
  4. Open the Finder app on your Mac and locate your device under the Locations tab.

How to update iOS and iPadOS with your Mac

Now that your iOS or iPadOS device is connected, you can use your Mac to install the latest software update or upgrade available.

  1. Open the Finder app on your Mac and locate your iOS or iPadOS device under the Locations tab.
  2. Click the Check for update button under the Software header.
  3. Choose either Download and install or Download only. The latter option won't install the update immediately and will save the installation file for a later time.
  4. Click the Next and Agree buttons that appear to learn more about the update and agree to the terms.
    2 Images
  5. Enter the password on your iOS or iPadOS device. Then click Continue on your Mac.
  6. Your update will download and install automatically. It's important to keep your device connected and keep your Mac connected to a network. You can track the update's progress at the bottom of the screen.

Should you always use your Mac to update your iOS or iPadOS device?

Since it can be a hassle to use your Mac to update your iOS or iPadOS device, it isn't a solution for every update. However, if you are low on storage space or need a quick download, your great Mac can be a big help. For the times when you want to prioritize convenience, your device can always download and install over-the-air updates, too.

Software updates can have a significant impact on your device, from adding vibrant new features to bringing critical security fixes. While older iPhone, iPod, and iPad software updates required a computer to be installed, many updates can now be applied over the air. That means all you need is your device to start the process automatically. However, it can be useful to install an update on another device. In some cases, it may be absolutely necessary. Luckily, you can use a Mac computer or laptop to update your iPhone or iPad to the latest version of iOS 17 or iPadOS 17.

Why you might need to use a Mac to update iOS

iOS 17's app library

Software updates, especially yearly upgrades, can come with extremely large file sizes. For example, the upgrade to iOS 17 has a file size of between 5 and 6GB. If you're using a base-model device with 64GB or 128GB of storage, you simply might not have the space to download the upgrade file if you have a lot of apps or data. However, since upgrade files are downloaded and subsequently deleted after the update is applied, it's not necessary to have them on-device.

To get around storage issues, you can download the update to your Mac — which probably has more available storage space — and install it to your iOS or iPadOS device via a cable. This might also be preferred if your Mac has an Ethernet connection, as you can download the update quicker than over a wireless network.

What you need to know before getting started

There are a few qualifications to updating your iOS or iPadOS device with a Mac. The computer in question must be running macOS Catalina or later, which was released in October 2019. Older Macs with earlier versions of macOS or Mac OS X may be able to update your device, but only in iTunes.

Before you update, you should back up your iOS or iPadOS device because there is always a risk of data loss when applying updates. Finally, make sure that your computer isn't using your phone's personal hotspot for a connection, as that'll will be terminated when your device restarts.

Connecting your iOS or iPadOS device to your Mac

Before you can install an update to your iOS or iPadOS device via your Mac, you need to get the devices connected. To do so, follow the steps below.

  1. Connect your iOS or iPadOS device to your Mac via a cable. The cable must be capable of data transfer.
  2. Follow the on-screen prompts on your Mac to allow your iPhone to connect. Click the Trust or Allow buttons as they appear.
  3. Follow the same steps on your iOS or iPadOS device. Press the Trust button as it appears, and enter your password or passcode.
  4. Open the Finder app on your Mac and locate your device under the Locations tab.

How to update iOS and iPadOS with your Mac

Now that your iOS or iPadOS device is connected, you can use your Mac to install the latest software update or upgrade available.

  1. Open the Finder app on your Mac and locate your iOS or iPadOS device under the Locations tab.
  2. Click the Check for update button under the Software header.
  3. Choose either Download and install or Download only. The latter option won't install the update immediately and will save the installation file for a later time.
  4. Click the Next and Agree buttons that appear to learn more about the update and agree to the terms.
    2 Images
  5. Enter the password on your iOS or iPadOS device. Then click Continue on your Mac.
  6. Your update will download and install automatically. It's important to keep your device connected and keep your Mac connected to a network. You can track the update's progress at the bottom of the screen.

Should you always use your Mac to update your iOS or iPadOS device?

Since it can be a hassle to use your Mac to update your iOS or iPadOS device, it isn't a solution for every update. However, if you are low on storage space or need a quick download, your great Mac can be a big help. For the times when you want to prioritize convenience, your device can always download and install over-the-air updates, too.

item.title: Best game controllers for Android in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best game controllers for Android in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Android has evolved into one of the best gaming platforms out there, and it's home to many serious mobile gamers playing various first-person shooters (FPS) and other fast-paced titles. You can play almost every game with on-screen controls, but using a dedicated controller can greatly improve your overall experience. Plenty of these modern titles, such as Call of Duty: Mobile and PUBG Mobile are best enjoyed with a controller. There's also an entire world of Android emulation that allows users to play games from older consoles.

If you're in the market to buy a new controller for your Android phone and are wondering which one to pick, you've come to the right place. We've compiled a list of the best game controllers you can buy for your Android smartphone. Let's dive in!

  • Source: Razer
    Razer Kishi V2
    Best overall
    $99 at Amazon
  • Source: SteelSeries
    SteelSeries Stratus Duo
    Affordable & durable
    $60 at Amazon
  • Gamesir X3
    Best controller with built-in cooling
    $100 at Amazon
  • Microsoft Xbox Core Controller
    Best all-around controller
    $50 at Microsoft
  • Razer Raiju Mobile
    Best controller with phone mount
    $30 at Amazon
  • Source: Sony
    Sony PlayStation DualSense Controller
    Best ergonomics
    $69 at Amazon
  • 8Bitdo Zero 2
    Best compact controller
    $20 at Amazon
  • MSI Force GC30V2 Wireless Gaming Controller
    Simple & affordable
    $36 at Amazon
  • Source: 8BitDo
    8Bitdo Pro 2
    Best for retro gaming
    $49 at Amazon
  • 8BitDo M30
    Best for Sega retro gaming
    $30 at Amazon

Our favorite game controllers for Android in 2023

Source: Razer
Razer Kishi V2
Best overall

Checks all the right boxes

The Razer Kishi v2 is the follow-up to the company's already-great universal controller. There are a few improvements across the board that makes it an excellent accessory for cloud gaming or emulation.

Pros
  • Programmable M2 bumpers
  • Fits more smartphones than ever
  • Low-latency input via USB-C
Cons
  • No headphone jack
  • Slightly bigger than the V1
  • Slow passthrough charging
$99 at Amazon $100 at Best Buy

Without a doubt, the Razer Kishi v2 is the best controller out there for Android phones. It works well with various smartphones and is very easy to set up and start gaming. Like the original Razer Kishi, the v2 model is one piece with left and right controllers that hook on the sides of your phone, allowing you to use your smartphone like a Nintendo Switch. The button layout on this controller is similar to the one you get on the Nintendo Switch Joy-Cons, and they're also very comfortable to use.

It also connects to your smartphone via USB-C, meaning latency should be greatly reduced compared to a regular Bluetooth controller. And through the USB-C port, you get pass-through charging to your phone since the Kishi doesn't need charging.

But there are a few caveats to buying this controller, including its relatively steep $99 price and its lack of cooling solutions. However, it's the most comfortable solution that will work with just about every phone. There's even a version now that works with iPhones. A good alternative to consider here is the Backbone One controller, which costs the same and has a similar form factor.

Source: SteelSeries
SteelSeries Stratus Duo
Affordable & durable

For those on a tight budget

The SteelSeries Stratus Duo is a great controller with solid build quality, clicky buttons, support for both Bluetooth and 2.4 GHz wireless, and more.

Pros
  • Solid build quality
  • Clicky and responsive buttons
  • Affordable price tag
Cons
  • Older Micro-USB port
  • Buttons are a bit hard to read
$60 at Amazon $60 at Best Buy

SteelSeries is one of the most popular names when it comes to gaming peripherals, and rightly so. The company has many high-quality and premium products under its belt, including some high-end game controllers for Android, too. The SteelSeries Stratus Duo is one of the best game controllers you can buy right now, standing up to the Razer Kishi, thanks to its lower price.

The SteelSeries Stratus Duo has a lot of things going for it, including excellent battery life, support for both Bluetooth and 2.4 GHz wireless (although we're more interested in Bluetooth here), and more. It also has a solid build quality and an ergonomic design that makes it very comfortable for gaming. It falls a bit short in some areas compared to the Razer Kishi V2 in that the buttons are a bit hard to read, and it comes with a Micro-USB port instead of USB-C.

Gamesir X3
Best controller with built-in cooling

The one with an RGB-equipped fan for serious gamers

GameSir X3 is a hybrid mobile game controller with a built-in cooler for your Android phone. It's bulkier than some other options out there, but it leaves a lot of room open for customization.

Pros
  • Built-in cooling
  • Adjustable USB-C connector
  • Tactile and responsive buttons
Cons
  • Bulkier than other controllers
  • Thumbsticks could've been bigger
$100 at Amazon

The original version of this article had the Gamesir X2 controller as one of our top picks, but it pales in comparison to the newer Gamesir X3. It may not be the best pick due to its high price and issues with the thumbsticks, but it's an impressive controller that comes with some thoughtful extras that didn't exist on the X2.

The highlight of the Gamesir X3 controller has got to be its built-in RGB-equipped fan and heatsink assembly. It's a powerful controller for serious smartphone gamers as it can cut CPU temperatures by over 10 degrees Celsius. It's a bit on the bulkier side, but it's ergonomic, offers many customization options, and will keep your phone and hands cool. You get two styles of D-Pad, and you can also customize the height of the joysticks. This particular game controller will turn some heads, so be sure to check it out.

Microsoft Xbox Core Controller
Best all-around controller

A simple controller that gets the job done

$50 $60 Save $10

The Xbox Core Controller is the one that ships with the Xbox Series S and Xbox Series X, and it comes with all the familiar features you might expect from an Xbox controller.

Pros
  • Affordable price tag
  • Simple and easy to use
  • Tactile and responsive buttons
Cons
  • Barebones design with no extras
  • Uses AA batteries
  • Need extra clip to attach to your phone
$55 at Amazon $50 at Microsoft

Sometimes the best options come where you least expect them, or if you're a console gamer, it may even come from what you already have. The Xbox Core Controller might be for the Xbox, but it supports and works flawlessly with Android phones. This is the standard Xbox controller bundled with the Xbox Series S and the Xbox Series X. It's a surprisingly solid controller for PC gaming, and it works out of the box with Android phones, too.

The Xbox Core Controller is as barebones as an Xbox controller can get, but it includes a slightly tweaked design from the previous generation. It's fully compatible with any game that supports it, and you can even get mount clips for mounting your phone to the controller. The best thing about the Xbox Core controller is that it's readily available to purchase on the market and comes in different color options.

Razer Raiju Mobile
Best controller with phone mount

A pro-gaming controller with an adjustable mount

The Razer Raiju Mobile is a pro mobile gaming controller with a familiar Xbox-like layout, Bluetooth connectivity, an integrated phone dock, and more.

Pros
  • Built-in phone mount
  • Familiar Xbox layout
  • Supports both wired and Bluetooth connections
Cons
  • Mounting a phone makes the controller top-heavy
  • Mount could make it difficult to adjust volume
$30 at Amazon

The Xbox Core controller works flawlessly with Android, but it doesn't have a built-in phone clip, meaning you have to set up your phone on a kickstand or buy a separate phone clip. The Razer Raiju Mobile Pro controller solves that problem by offering a built-in phone mount. The layout of the Raiju Mobile Pro is also very similar to the Xbox Core controller, so you'll feel right at home coming to this from a traditional Core controller.

The Raiju Mobile controller also offers additional buttons and a hair trigger mode for a competitive advantage. The tactile action buttons offer a satisfying click, and it has support for both wired and wireless connections. The Raiju Mobile Pro controller is also more affordable than the traditional Xbox Core controller, so it's worth considering if you want to try something other than a basic Xbox controller.

Source: Sony
Sony PlayStation DualSense Controller
Best ergonomics

Looks good and has great ergonomics

Sony's new DualSesne sets a new benchmark for game controllers. Its adaptive triggers and haptics don't work on Android, but it's a great controller to hold and use.

Pros
  • Built-in rechargeable battery
  • Solid design and build quality
  • Built-in mic
Cons
  • Battery life could've been better
  • A bit on the expensive side
  • Don't get PS5-exclusive features on phone
$69 at Amazon $70 at Best Buy

Sony's PlayStation controllers also work well with various other devices, including Android. Yes, you will miss out on some PS5-exclusive features, but there are no compatibility issues, and it works flawlessly out of the box with an Android phone. Sony's DualSense controller connects to Android phones via Bluetooth and works just like any other controller mentioned in this list.

It goes without saying that you won't experience some rich features like the adaptive triggers or the haptics, but everything else will work as intended. The PS5 DualSense controller has an amazing ergonomic and refreshed design over the older DualShock 4. It's one of the best controllers out there right now, and it's worth considering if you're a serious smartphone gamer. It also works with almost all Android games, including some of the major titles, so you'll have no issues in terms of usability as well.

8Bitdo Zero 2
Best compact controller

Easy to carry, and works with all major platforms

The 8Bitdo Zero 2 is one of the most compact Bluetooth controllers out there on the market. It's available in a bunch of different colors, and it also works with a variety of other platforms, including Windows, macOS, and more.

Pros
  • Affordable price tag
  • Compact form-factor
  • Works with a variety of devices
Cons
  • Not the most premium-looking controller
  • Shape and size are not suitable for everyone
$20 at Amazon

If you want a compact Bluetooth controller to pair with your Android phone, you must check out the 8Bitdo Zero 2. It's undoubtedly one of the most portable controllers, and it doesn't skimp on any essentials despite its form factor. The 8Bitdo Zero 2 is one of those controllers you can throw in your pocket before stepping out of your home. It weighs only 20 grams, so it might be one of the lightest game controllers.

One of the best things about this particular controller is that it's compatible with many other platforms, including Nintendo Switch, Windows, macOS, and Android phones, so you'll get access to all the features. It can last for around 8 hours on a single charge, and it's available to purchase in various colors, too.

MSI Force GC30V2 Wireless Gaming Controller
Simple & affordable

Among the cheapest options for casual gamers

$36 $40 Save $4

The Force GC30 V2 is an upgraded version of the original GC30 model that brings a lot of improvements. For one, it comes in an optional white color, and you also get an interchangeable D-pad, dual vibration motors, and more.

Pros
  • Affordable price tag
  • Has same layout as an Xbox controller
  • Dual connection modes
Cons
  • Only compatible with PC and Android
  • Needs extra phone clip to attach to phone
$36 at Amazon

The MSI Force GC30 V2, as the name implies, is the upgraded version of the original GC30 controller that we highlighted as the most affordable pick here previously. This updated variant slots right in as the "Best affordable pick" because it's the cheapest of this lot. This once again costs a bit more than the 8Bit Zero 2, but you also get a full-sized controller with a solid button layout.

Unlike the older one, the GC30 V2 comes in both black and white colors, but it retains the same Xbox layout and build that we know and like. This new model comes with dual vibration motors, an interchangeable D-pad, and support for the dual connection. It works with both Android and PC, so be sure to check it out if you're only looking for a simple controller to get the job done.

Source: 8BitDo
8Bitdo Pro 2
Best for retro gaming

For those who enjoy retro games on Android

The 8Bitdo Pro 2 is one of the best controllers out there for those who want to do some retro gaming. It's available in three finishes, and they're all compatible with a lot of platforms, including Android, macOS, Windows, and more.

Pros
  • Programmable options
  • Solid build quality
  • Works with a variety of devices
Cons
  • Requires an additional phone mount
$49 at Amazon $50 at Best Buy

If you love playing retro games on your Android phone using an emulator, then you probably already know how annoying touch controls can be. Modern controllers sometimes won't play nice with older titles, which is where the 8BitDo Pro 2 comes in. Think of it as a Super Nintendo controller on steroids — it's wireless, features two joysticks, and is a tad bit more ergonomic. The 8BitDo Pro 2 is actually very similar to the Nintendo Switch's Pro Controller in a lot of ways, including button layout and joysticks.

If you're going to do a lot of retro gaming on your smartphone, the 8BitDo Pro 2 is probably the best game controller to get. It's not the most affordable controller in this particular list, but it offers good value for $50 and works well with various devices.

8BitDo M30
Best for Sega retro gaming

The one with a unique layout for older Sega games

The 8BitDo M30 is just as good of an option as the Pro 2, but it features a SEGA Genesis button layout, making it ideal to play games from SEGA consoles from that era on your smartphone.

Pros
  • Classic design
  • Affordable price tag
  • Works well over Bluetooth
Cons
  • Not the best controller to play modern games
$30 at Amazon

If you're planning to play some retro games that came out on Sega consoles, particularly the Sega Genesis, then the 8BitDo M30 is the one to get. This controller comes with a unique 6-button and D-pad layout that's ideal for playing older Sega console games. You don't get the same experience playing those titles on one of the modern controllers.

It's easy to set up and connects to your phone via Bluetooth. This should give you way better compatibility with your games, allowing you to play them how you're supposed to. It's also a great option for those who play Sega games through Nintendo Switch Online.

Best game controller for Android in 2023: The bottom line

We've highlighted a lot of different controllers that work well with Android phones, but the Razer Kishi V2 is the one that checks all the right boxes. It has improved a lot over its predecessor, and it now works well with more devices than ever. It's not perfect, as it lacks a headphone jack and has slow pass-through charging, but it's still better than many other options mentioned in this collection.

Source: Razer
Razer Kishi V2
Editor's Choice

The Razer Kishi v2 is the follow-up to the company's already-great universal controller. There are a few improvements across the board that makes it an excellent cloud gaming or emulation controller.

$99 at Amazon $100 at Best Buy

The SteelSeries Stratus Duo comes close to matching the general performance and user experience of the Kishi V2, but it lacks some essentials like a USB-C port. That being said, it'll save you some money compared to the Kishi. We've also added some other options to this list, including the 8Bitdo Pro 2 for retro gamer fans, so be sure to check them out. Alternatively, you can also pair your Android phone with your existing Xbox or the Sony DualSense controller and save money if you don't want to splurge on a controller exclusively for smartphones. Note that some controllers would need a clipping mount as well, so here's a list of the best smartphone mounts for controllers.

It works with both Android and PC, so be sure to check it out if you're only looking for a simple controller to get the job done.

Source: 8BitDo
8Bitdo Pro 2
Best for retro gaming

For those who enjoy retro games on Android

The 8Bitdo Pro 2 is one of the best controllers out there for those who want to do some retro gaming. It's available in three finishes, and they're all compatible with a lot of platforms, including Android, macOS, Windows, and more.

Pros
  • Programmable options
  • Solid build quality
  • Works with a variety of devices
Cons
  • Requires an additional phone mount
$49 at Amazon $50 at Best Buy

If you love playing retro games on your Android phone using an emulator, then you probably already know how annoying touch controls can be. Modern controllers sometimes won't play nice with older titles, which is where the 8BitDo Pro 2 comes in. Think of it as a Super Nintendo controller on steroids — it's wireless, features two joysticks, and is a tad bit more ergonomic. The 8BitDo Pro 2 is actually very similar to the Nintendo Switch's Pro Controller in a lot of ways, including button layout and joysticks.

If you're going to do a lot of retro gaming on your smartphone, the 8BitDo Pro 2 is probably the best game controller to get. It's not the most affordable controller in this particular list, but it offers good value for $50 and works well with various devices.

8BitDo M30
Best for Sega retro gaming

The one with a unique layout for older Sega games

The 8BitDo M30 is just as good of an option as the Pro 2, but it features a SEGA Genesis button layout, making it ideal to play games from SEGA consoles from that era on your smartphone.

Pros
  • Classic design
  • Affordable price tag
  • Works well over Bluetooth
Cons
  • Not the best controller to play modern games
$30 at Amazon

If you're planning to play some retro games that came out on Sega consoles, particularly the Sega Genesis, then the 8BitDo M30 is the one to get. This controller comes with a unique 6-button and D-pad layout that's ideal for playing older Sega console games. You don't get the same experience playing those titles on one of the modern controllers.

It's easy to set up and connects to your phone via Bluetooth. This should give you way better compatibility with your games, allowing you to play them how you're supposed to. It's also a great option for those who play Sega games through Nintendo Switch Online.

Best game controller for Android in 2023: The bottom line

We've highlighted a lot of different controllers that work well with Android phones, but the Razer Kishi V2 is the one that checks all the right boxes. It has improved a lot over its predecessor, and it now works well with more devices than ever. It's not perfect, as it lacks a headphone jack and has slow pass-through charging, but it's still better than many other options mentioned in this collection.

Source: Razer
Razer Kishi V2
Editor's Choice

The Razer Kishi v2 is the follow-up to the company's already-great universal controller. There are a few improvements across the board that makes it an excellent cloud gaming or emulation controller.

$99 at Amazon $100 at Best Buy

The SteelSeries Stratus Duo comes close to matching the general performance and user experience of the Kishi V2, but it lacks some essentials like a USB-C port. That being said, it'll save you some money compared to the Kishi. We've also added some other options to this list, including the 8Bitdo Pro 2 for retro gamer fans, so be sure to check them out. Alternatively, you can also pair your Android phone with your existing Xbox or the Sony DualSense controller and save money if you don't want to splurge on a controller exclusively for smartphones. Note that some controllers would need a clipping mount as well, so here's a list of the best smartphone mounts for controllers.
item.content:encoded:
Android has evolved into one of the best gaming platforms out there, and it's home to many serious mobile gamers playing various first-person shooters (FPS) and other fast-paced titles. You can play almost every game with on-screen controls, but using a dedicated controller can greatly improve your overall experience. Plenty of these modern titles, such as Call of Duty: Mobile and PUBG Mobile are best enjoyed with a controller. There's also an entire world of Android emulation that allows users to play games from older consoles.

If you're in the market to buy a new controller for your Android phone and are wondering which one to pick, you've come to the right place. We've compiled a list of the best game controllers you can buy for your Android smartphone. Let's dive in!

  • Source: Razer
    Razer Kishi V2
    Best overall
    $99 at Amazon
  • Source: SteelSeries
    SteelSeries Stratus Duo
    Affordable & durable
    $60 at Amazon
  • Gamesir X3
    Best controller with built-in cooling
    $100 at Amazon
  • Microsoft Xbox Core Controller
    Best all-around controller
    $50 at Microsoft
  • Razer Raiju Mobile
    Best controller with phone mount
    $30 at Amazon
  • Source: Sony
    Sony PlayStation DualSense Controller
    Best ergonomics
    $69 at Amazon
  • 8Bitdo Zero 2
    Best compact controller
    $20 at Amazon
  • MSI Force GC30V2 Wireless Gaming Controller
    Simple & affordable
    $36 at Amazon
  • Source: 8BitDo
    8Bitdo Pro 2
    Best for retro gaming
    $49 at Amazon
  • 8BitDo M30
    Best for Sega retro gaming
    $30 at Amazon

Our favorite game controllers for Android in 2023

Source: Razer
Razer Kishi V2
Best overall

Checks all the right boxes

The Razer Kishi v2 is the follow-up to the company's already-great universal controller. There are a few improvements across the board that makes it an excellent accessory for cloud gaming or emulation.

Pros
  • Programmable M2 bumpers
  • Fits more smartphones than ever
  • Low-latency input via USB-C
Cons
  • No headphone jack
  • Slightly bigger than the V1
  • Slow passthrough charging
$99 at Amazon $100 at Best Buy

Without a doubt, the Razer Kishi v2 is the best controller out there for Android phones. It works well with various smartphones and is very easy to set up and start gaming. Like the original Razer Kishi, the v2 model is one piece with left and right controllers that hook on the sides of your phone, allowing you to use your smartphone like a Nintendo Switch. The button layout on this controller is similar to the one you get on the Nintendo Switch Joy-Cons, and they're also very comfortable to use.

It also connects to your smartphone via USB-C, meaning latency should be greatly reduced compared to a regular Bluetooth controller. And through the USB-C port, you get pass-through charging to your phone since the Kishi doesn't need charging.

But there are a few caveats to buying this controller, including its relatively steep $99 price and its lack of cooling solutions. However, it's the most comfortable solution that will work with just about every phone. There's even a version now that works with iPhones. A good alternative to consider here is the Backbone One controller, which costs the same and has a similar form factor.

Source: SteelSeries
SteelSeries Stratus Duo
Affordable & durable

For those on a tight budget

The SteelSeries Stratus Duo is a great controller with solid build quality, clicky buttons, support for both Bluetooth and 2.4 GHz wireless, and more.

Pros
  • Solid build quality
  • Clicky and responsive buttons
  • Affordable price tag
Cons
  • Older Micro-USB port
  • Buttons are a bit hard to read
$60 at Amazon $60 at Best Buy

SteelSeries is one of the most popular names when it comes to gaming peripherals, and rightly so. The company has many high-quality and premium products under its belt, including some high-end game controllers for Android, too. The SteelSeries Stratus Duo is one of the best game controllers you can buy right now, standing up to the Razer Kishi, thanks to its lower price.

The SteelSeries Stratus Duo has a lot of things going for it, including excellent battery life, support for both Bluetooth and 2.4 GHz wireless (although we're more interested in Bluetooth here), and more. It also has a solid build quality and an ergonomic design that makes it very comfortable for gaming. It falls a bit short in some areas compared to the Razer Kishi V2 in that the buttons are a bit hard to read, and it comes with a Micro-USB port instead of USB-C.

Gamesir X3
Best controller with built-in cooling

The one with an RGB-equipped fan for serious gamers

GameSir X3 is a hybrid mobile game controller with a built-in cooler for your Android phone. It's bulkier than some other options out there, but it leaves a lot of room open for customization.

Pros
  • Built-in cooling
  • Adjustable USB-C connector
  • Tactile and responsive buttons
Cons
  • Bulkier than other controllers
  • Thumbsticks could've been bigger
$100 at Amazon

The original version of this article had the Gamesir X2 controller as one of our top picks, but it pales in comparison to the newer Gamesir X3. It may not be the best pick due to its high price and issues with the thumbsticks, but it's an impressive controller that comes with some thoughtful extras that didn't exist on the X2.

The highlight of the Gamesir X3 controller has got to be its built-in RGB-equipped fan and heatsink assembly. It's a powerful controller for serious smartphone gamers as it can cut CPU temperatures by over 10 degrees Celsius. It's a bit on the bulkier side, but it's ergonomic, offers many customization options, and will keep your phone and hands cool. You get two styles of D-Pad, and you can also customize the height of the joysticks. This particular game controller will turn some heads, so be sure to check it out.

Microsoft Xbox Core Controller
Best all-around controller

A simple controller that gets the job done

$50 $60 Save $10

The Xbox Core Controller is the one that ships with the Xbox Series S and Xbox Series X, and it comes with all the familiar features you might expect from an Xbox controller.

Pros
  • Affordable price tag
  • Simple and easy to use
  • Tactile and responsive buttons
Cons
  • Barebones design with no extras
  • Uses AA batteries
  • Need extra clip to attach to your phone
$55 at Amazon $50 at Microsoft

Sometimes the best options come where you least expect them, or if you're a console gamer, it may even come from what you already have. The Xbox Core Controller might be for the Xbox, but it supports and works flawlessly with Android phones. This is the standard Xbox controller bundled with the Xbox Series S and the Xbox Series X. It's a surprisingly solid controller for PC gaming, and it works out of the box with Android phones, too.

The Xbox Core Controller is as barebones as an Xbox controller can get, but it includes a slightly tweaked design from the previous generation. It's fully compatible with any game that supports it, and you can even get mount clips for mounting your phone to the controller. The best thing about the Xbox Core controller is that it's readily available to purchase on the market and comes in different color options.

Razer Raiju Mobile
Best controller with phone mount

A pro-gaming controller with an adjustable mount

The Razer Raiju Mobile is a pro mobile gaming controller with a familiar Xbox-like layout, Bluetooth connectivity, an integrated phone dock, and more.

Pros
  • Built-in phone mount
  • Familiar Xbox layout
  • Supports both wired and Bluetooth connections
Cons
  • Mounting a phone makes the controller top-heavy
  • Mount could make it difficult to adjust volume
$30 at Amazon

The Xbox Core controller works flawlessly with Android, but it doesn't have a built-in phone clip, meaning you have to set up your phone on a kickstand or buy a separate phone clip. The Razer Raiju Mobile Pro controller solves that problem by offering a built-in phone mount. The layout of the Raiju Mobile Pro is also very similar to the Xbox Core controller, so you'll feel right at home coming to this from a traditional Core controller.

The Raiju Mobile controller also offers additional buttons and a hair trigger mode for a competitive advantage. The tactile action buttons offer a satisfying click, and it has support for both wired and wireless connections. The Raiju Mobile Pro controller is also more affordable than the traditional Xbox Core controller, so it's worth considering if you want to try something other than a basic Xbox controller.

Source: Sony
Sony PlayStation DualSense Controller
Best ergonomics

Looks good and has great ergonomics

Sony's new DualSesne sets a new benchmark for game controllers. Its adaptive triggers and haptics don't work on Android, but it's a great controller to hold and use.

Pros
  • Built-in rechargeable battery
  • Solid design and build quality
  • Built-in mic
Cons
  • Battery life could've been better
  • A bit on the expensive side
  • Don't get PS5-exclusive features on phone
$69 at Amazon $70 at Best Buy

Sony's PlayStation controllers also work well with various other devices, including Android. Yes, you will miss out on some PS5-exclusive features, but there are no compatibility issues, and it works flawlessly out of the box with an Android phone. Sony's DualSense controller connects to Android phones via Bluetooth and works just like any other controller mentioned in this list.

It goes without saying that you won't experience some rich features like the adaptive triggers or the haptics, but everything else will work as intended. The PS5 DualSense controller has an amazing ergonomic and refreshed design over the older DualShock 4. It's one of the best controllers out there right now, and it's worth considering if you're a serious smartphone gamer. It also works with almost all Android games, including some of the major titles, so you'll have no issues in terms of usability as well.

8Bitdo Zero 2
Best compact controller

Easy to carry, and works with all major platforms

The 8Bitdo Zero 2 is one of the most compact Bluetooth controllers out there on the market. It's available in a bunch of different colors, and it also works with a variety of other platforms, including Windows, macOS, and more.

Pros
  • Affordable price tag
  • Compact form-factor
  • Works with a variety of devices
Cons
  • Not the most premium-looking controller
  • Shape and size are not suitable for everyone
$20 at Amazon

If you want a compact Bluetooth controller to pair with your Android phone, you must check out the 8Bitdo Zero 2. It's undoubtedly one of the most portable controllers, and it doesn't skimp on any essentials despite its form factor. The 8Bitdo Zero 2 is one of those controllers you can throw in your pocket before stepping out of your home. It weighs only 20 grams, so it might be one of the lightest game controllers.

One of the best things about this particular controller is that it's compatible with many other platforms, including Nintendo Switch, Windows, macOS, and Android phones, so you'll get access to all the features. It can last for around 8 hours on a single charge, and it's available to purchase in various colors, too.

MSI Force GC30V2 Wireless Gaming Controller
Simple & affordable

Among the cheapest options for casual gamers

$36 $40 Save $4

The Force GC30 V2 is an upgraded version of the original GC30 model that brings a lot of improvements. For one, it comes in an optional white color, and you also get an interchangeable D-pad, dual vibration motors, and more.

Pros
  • Affordable price tag
  • Has same layout as an Xbox controller
  • Dual connection modes
Cons
  • Only compatible with PC and Android
  • Needs extra phone clip to attach to phone
$36 at Amazon

The MSI Force GC30 V2, as the name implies, is the upgraded version of the original GC30 controller that we highlighted as the most affordable pick here previously. This updated variant slots right in as the "Best affordable pick" because it's the cheapest of this lot. This once again costs a bit more than the 8Bit Zero 2, but you also get a full-sized controller with a solid button layout.

Unlike the older one, the GC30 V2 comes in both black and white colors, but it retains the same Xbox layout and build that we know and like. This new model comes with dual vibration motors, an interchangeable D-pad, and support for the dual connection. It works with both Android and PC, so be sure to check it out if you're only looking for a simple controller to get the job done.

Source: 8BitDo
8Bitdo Pro 2
Best for retro gaming

For those who enjoy retro games on Android

The 8Bitdo Pro 2 is one of the best controllers out there for those who want to do some retro gaming. It's available in three finishes, and they're all compatible with a lot of platforms, including Android, macOS, Windows, and more.

Pros
  • Programmable options
  • Solid build quality
  • Works with a variety of devices
Cons
  • Requires an additional phone mount
$49 at Amazon $50 at Best Buy

Modern controllers sometimes won't play nice with older titles, which is where the 8BitDo Pro 2 comes in. The one with a unique layout for older Sega games

Pros
  • Classic design
  • Affordable price tag
  • Works well over Bluetooth
Cons
  • Not the best controller to play modern games
$30 at Amazon

If you're planning to play some retro games that came out on Sega consoles, particularly the Sega Genesis, then the 8BitDo M30 is the one to get. This controller comes with a unique 6-button and D-pad layout that's ideal for playing older Sega console games. Best game controller for Android in 2023: The bottom line

It has improved a lot over its predecessor, and it now works well with more devices than ever. Source: Razer
Razer Kishi V2
Editor's Choice

The Razer Kishi v2 is the follow-up to the company's already-great universal controller. There are a few improvements across the board that makes it an excellent cloud gaming or emulation controller.

$99 at Amazon $100 at Best Buy

The SteelSeries Stratus Duo comes close to matching the general performance and user experience of the Kishi V2, but it lacks some essentials like a USB-C port. item.title: Dolphin Emulator: What you should know about GameCube and Wii emulation
item.itunes:title: Dolphin Emulator: What you should know about GameCube and Wii emulation
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • Setting up Dolphin Emulator
  • Performance problems? Try Dolphin MMJR/MMJR2
  • Enhance your game
  • Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: Wii
  • Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: GameCube

Ever wanted to play GameCube and Wii games on your phone? I'm sure you have, and Dolphin Emulator is the best way to do so. It has an ever-growing compatibility list, works on all kinds of smartphones depending on what game you want to play, and is the best way to enjoy classic titles on those Nintendo systems. It's available on Android, but you can also get it on Windows, Mac, and Linux.

If you want to download Dolphin Emulator, you can follow the Google Play Store link below. Otherwise, be sure to check out the website for more frequent builds.

Setting up Dolphin Emulator

Setting up Dolphin Emulator is fairly easy, and it works out of the box on most configurations. Install it, get your game files, and launch. That's really all you need in most cases. Any specific changes you make will likely be to the detriment of some other core aspect of the game because Dolphin Emulator is shipped with default settings for the most optimal performance, so there's no need to tweak settings for the most part.

For now, any device that has a Qualcomm Snapdragon 845 or newer should do the job for basic emulation, but the newer the chip, the better the performance. Games that don't require as much processing power will still work fine on a Snapdragon 845, but you should also be mindful of thermal throttling. Thermal throttling is when your phone decreases the amount of work it completes in order to cool down, which can have a detrimental effect on performance as your phone heats up. Using any of the best Android phones available on the market today will be more than good enough. Given that we're now far ahead of the Snapdragon 845, you'll be able to benefit from resolution upscaling to 2x, 3x, or even 4x in a lot of titles.

Once you have Dolphin Emulator set up, you'll need to copy over your game ISOs to your smartphone. We do not condone piracy or the illegal sharing of games, and you'll need to dump your own titles to be able to play them on your smartphone. You can rip games specifically for use on Dolphin Emulator by following this official guide on the official Dolphin Emulator website. You can do it with a hacked Nintendo Wii, or with some older DVD readers and a computer. Dolphin Emulator can also play WAD files (Wii Channel files), and that guide also shows you how to dump those, too.

Performance problems? Try Dolphin MMJR/MMJR2

If you're having performance problems with the original emulator, give Dolphin MMJR/MMJR2 a try. MMJR 11505 and MMJR2 17878 are the two most used ones from that archived link, but at this stage, there isn't really a need to use either of these. If you are having performance problems with the latest versions of Dolphin Emulator, then chances are you just need to upgrade your phone.

MMJR is a fork of the official emulator with some hacks and workarounds for common problems found on Android smartphones, particularly with buggy GPU drivers. It's best used on smartphones with Mali GPUs as, on Android, it's hard to have a good emulation experience on non-Qualcomm chips, but the latest official versions of Dolphin should be fine. MMJR2 is built on top of the official Dolphin Source code (whereas MMJR is built on the older MMJ code), and as such, while it may be more accurate, it may also perform worse on non-Snapdragon chipsets. You'll need to uninstall the original Dolphin Emulator before installing the MMJR version, as they have different build certificates, and it'll fail to install over the original.

We don't recommend using MMJR/MMJR2 overall but instead suggest that they're as a last resort on older devices only.

Enhance your game

You can improve your gaming experience with some of Dolphin's enhancements in the settings. A common one for people to do is to bump up the internal resolution, as usually, it's the CPU that causes slowdowns and other problems. The GPU is rarely pushed to its limit, so you can often increase the graphical fidelity by increasing the rendering resolution without any effect on performance.

There are a few settings that you might want to modify, but most of them are purely cosmetic.

Above are the only settings that you'll want to touch. You can change the video backend from "OpenGL" to "Vulkan" (though Vulkan support may not be the greatest), and you can also enable an FPS counter so that you can see how well your game is running. If you want to change the internal resolution and set up anti-aliasing, you can also do that, though keep in mind that bumping up may cause performance problems. You can force widescreen in games that don't support it, and you can disable fog in other games, too. Finally, you can also make some Wii or GameCube-specific changes for emulation, particularly when it comes to the Wii remote.

Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: Wii

Mario Kart Wii

Mario Kart Wii is an example of a game that plays well on Android, and with the latest Qualcomm chipsets (such as the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Plus Gen 1), you'll have fantastic performance. Touch controls get a bit finicky when it comes to Mario Kart Wii, though you can make it work by clawing your hand in the way that I was playing the game above. I definitely recommend just picking up a controller instead, though. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Super Smash Bros: Brawl

Super Smash Bros: Brawl on the OPPO Find X3 Pro

Super Smash Bros: Brawl runs very well on a smartphone, even if the controls are a bit finicky. For this one, I'd recommend hooking up an actual controller to your phone to play, as the touch inputs can be difficult to use for such a fast-paced game. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: GameCube

The Simpsons: Hit & Run

The Simpsons: Hit & Run is a childhood favorite for many people, and the game runs very well on the latest and greatest Qualcomm chipsets. While lag is still evident in certain sections, it's certainly not unplayable. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker

The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker is one of the highest-rated games on the GameCube, and it runs near-flawlessly on the OPPO Find X3 Pro. Performance is superb, and the game runs very well, even with a bumped-up internal resolution. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Relive your childhood with Wii and GameCube games on the go

I, for one, love being able to play GameCube and Wii games wherever I go, especially with the wide variety of supported games that you can play nowadays. While performance is nowhere near perfect, it's absolutely worth giving it a try if you feel some nostalgia for older games. I often enjoy playing The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker and The Simpsons: Hit & Run, and it's even better knowing that I can take them with me whenever I want. Definitely give it a try, as you might be surprised by how well your favorite games run!

Mario Kart Wii

Mario Kart Wii is an example of a game that plays well on Android, and with the latest Qualcomm chipsets (such as the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Plus Gen 1), you'll have fantastic performance. Touch controls get a bit finicky when it comes to Mario Kart Wii, though you can make it work by clawing your hand in the way that I was playing the game above. I definitely recommend just picking up a controller instead, though. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Super Smash Bros: Brawl

Super Smash Bros: Brawl on the OPPO Find X3 Pro

Super Smash Bros: Brawl runs very well on a smartphone, even if the controls are a bit finicky. For this one, I'd recommend hooking up an actual controller to your phone to play, as the touch inputs can be difficult to use for such a fast-paced game. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: GameCube

The Simpsons: Hit & Run

The Simpsons: Hit & Run is a childhood favorite for many people, and the game runs very well on the latest and greatest Qualcomm chipsets. While lag is still evident in certain sections, it's certainly not unplayable. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker

The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker is one of the highest-rated games on the GameCube, and it runs near-flawlessly on the OPPO Find X3 Pro. Performance is superb, and the game runs very well, even with a bumped-up internal resolution. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Relive your childhood with Wii and GameCube games on the go

I, for one, love being able to play GameCube and Wii games wherever I go, especially with the wide variety of supported games that you can play nowadays. While performance is nowhere near perfect, it's absolutely worth giving it a try if you feel some nostalgia for older games. I often enjoy playing The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker and The Simpsons: Hit & Run, and it's even better knowing that I can take them with me whenever I want. Definitely give it a try, as you might be surprised by how well your favorite games run!
item.content:encoded:
Quick Links

  • Setting up Dolphin Emulator
  • Performance problems? Try Dolphin MMJR/MMJR2
  • Enhance your game
  • Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: Wii
  • Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: GameCube

Ever wanted to play GameCube and Wii games on your phone? I'm sure you have, and Dolphin Emulator is the best way to do so. It has an ever-growing compatibility list, works on all kinds of smartphones depending on what game you want to play, and is the best way to enjoy classic titles on those Nintendo systems. It's available on Android, but you can also get it on Windows, Mac, and Linux.

If you want to download Dolphin Emulator, you can follow the Google Play Store link below. Otherwise, be sure to check out the website for more frequent builds.

Setting up Dolphin Emulator

Setting up Dolphin Emulator is fairly easy, and it works out of the box on most configurations. Install it, get your game files, and launch. That's really all you need in most cases. Any specific changes you make will likely be to the detriment of some other core aspect of the game because Dolphin Emulator is shipped with default settings for the most optimal performance, so there's no need to tweak settings for the most part.

For now, any device that has a Qualcomm Snapdragon 845 or newer should do the job for basic emulation, but the newer the chip, the better the performance. Games that don't require as much processing power will still work fine on a Snapdragon 845, but you should also be mindful of thermal throttling. Thermal throttling is when your phone decreases the amount of work it completes in order to cool down, which can have a detrimental effect on performance as your phone heats up. Using any of the best Android phones available on the market today will be more than good enough. Given that we're now far ahead of the Snapdragon 845, you'll be able to benefit from resolution upscaling to 2x, 3x, or even 4x in a lot of titles.

Once you have Dolphin Emulator set up, you'll need to copy over your game ISOs to your smartphone. We do not condone piracy or the illegal sharing of games, and you'll need to dump your own titles to be able to play them on your smartphone. You can rip games specifically for use on Dolphin Emulator by following this official guide on the official Dolphin Emulator website. You can do it with a hacked Nintendo Wii, or with some older DVD readers and a computer. Dolphin Emulator can also play WAD files (Wii Channel files), and that guide also shows you how to dump those, too.

Performance problems? Try Dolphin MMJR/MMJR2

If you're having performance problems with the original emulator, give Dolphin MMJR/MMJR2 a try. MMJR 11505 and MMJR2 17878 are the two most used ones from that archived link, but at this stage, there isn't really a need to use either of these. If you are having performance problems with the latest versions of Dolphin Emulator, then chances are you just need to upgrade your phone.

MMJR is a fork of the official emulator with some hacks and workarounds for common problems found on Android smartphones, particularly with buggy GPU drivers. It's best used on smartphones with Mali GPUs as, on Android, it's hard to have a good emulation experience on non-Qualcomm chips, but the latest official versions of Dolphin should be fine. MMJR2 is built on top of the official Dolphin Source code (whereas MMJR is built on the older MMJ code), and as such, while it may be more accurate, it may also perform worse on non-Snapdragon chipsets. You'll need to uninstall the original Dolphin Emulator before installing the MMJR version, as they have different build certificates, and it'll fail to install over the original.

We don't recommend using MMJR/MMJR2 overall but instead suggest that they're as a last resort on older devices only.

Enhance your game

You can improve your gaming experience with some of Dolphin's enhancements in the settings. A common one for people to do is to bump up the internal resolution, as usually, it's the CPU that causes slowdowns and other problems. The GPU is rarely pushed to its limit, so you can often increase the graphical fidelity by increasing the rendering resolution without any effect on performance.

There are a few settings that you might want to modify, but most of them are purely cosmetic.

Above are the only settings that you'll want to touch. You can change the video backend from "OpenGL" to "Vulkan" (though Vulkan support may not be the greatest), and you can also enable an FPS counter so that you can see how well your game is running. If you want to change the internal resolution and set up anti-aliasing, you can also do that, though keep in mind that bumping up may cause performance problems. You can force widescreen in games that don't support it, and you can disable fog in other games, too. Finally, you can also make some Wii or GameCube-specific changes for emulation, particularly when it comes to the Wii remote.

Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: Wii

Mario Kart Wii

Mario Kart Wii is an example of a game that plays well on Android, and with the latest Qualcomm chipsets (such as the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Plus Gen 1), you'll have fantastic performance. Touch controls get a bit finicky when it comes to Mario Kart Wii, though you can make it work by clawing your hand in the way that I was playing the game above. I definitely recommend just picking up a controller instead, though. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Super Smash Bros: Brawl

Super Smash Bros: Brawl on the OPPO Find X3 Pro

Super Smash Bros: Brawl runs very well on a smartphone, even if the controls are a bit finicky. For this one, I'd recommend hooking up an actual controller to your phone to play, as the touch inputs can be difficult to use for such a fast-paced game. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Games you can play with Dolphin Emulator: GameCube

The Simpsons: Hit & Run

The Simpsons: Hit & Run is a childhood favorite for many people, and the game runs very well on the latest and greatest Qualcomm chipsets. While lag is still evident in certain sections, it's certainly not unplayable. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker

The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker is one of the highest-rated games on the GameCube, and it runs near-flawlessly on the OPPO Find X3 Pro. Performance is superb, and the game runs very well, even with a bumped-up internal resolution. This game is running at 1920x1584, three times its original resolution.

Relive your childhood with Wii and GameCube games on the go

I, for one, love being able to play GameCube and Wii games wherever I go, especially with the wide variety of supported games that you can play nowadays. While performance is nowhere near perfect, it's absolutely worth giving it a try if you feel some nostalgia for older games. I often enjoy playing The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker and The Simpsons: Hit & Run, and it's even better knowing that I can take them with me whenever I want. Definitely give it a try, as you might be surprised by how well your favorite games run!

item.title: iPhone 15 vs iPhone 14 Pro: Get the new or stick with the old?
item.itunes:title: iPhone 15 vs iPhone 14 Pro: Get the new or stick with the old?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
• iPhone 15
    New kid on the block

    The Apple iPhone 15 comes with A16 Bionic chipset, a 6.1-inch OLED display, and a 48MP primary camera. Among the new features here are a USB Type-C port and Dynamic Island. There's also a special portrait shooting mode that lets you adjust the focus after shooting. 

    Pros
    • Finally has Dynamic Island
    • Special portrait shooting mode
    • Aluminum frame with contoured edges
    Cons
    • Slow 60Hz display
    • USB Type-C port only supports USB 2.0 speeds
    • Storage only up to 512GB
    $830 at Best Buy
  • Source: Apple
    Apple iPhone 14 Pro
    Still going strong

    Apple iPhone 14 Pro is one of the advanced iPhones from the last generation, giving you a 120Hz refresh rate display and a triple camera setup. The phone comes with a variety of photography and videography options thanks to support for Apple ProRAW and ProRes. It still stands tall as compared to the iPhone 15.  

    Pros
    • 120Hz ProMotion display
    • Impressive camera and video modes
    • Storage options up to 1TB
    Cons
    • Heavier
    • Being discontinued
    $899 at Best Buy $879 at Amazon

Key Takeaways

  • The iPhone 15 and iPhone 14 Pro have similar dimensions and displays, but the iPhone 14 Pro has a slightly heavier build and a triple camera setup.
  • Both phones come with the A16 Bionic chip and have similar performance and storage options, but the iPhone 14 Pro offers up to 1TB of storage.
  • The iPhone 14 Pro has a few advantages in the camera department, including a telephoto lens, ProRAW and macro modes, and ProRes video shooting.

Apple recently announced its iPhone 15 lineup. While the Pro models come with a brand-new chipset, the iPhone 15 and 15 Plus come with a mix of new features and some compromises. The presence of Dynamic Island and the USB Type-C port are the biggest changes in the regular iPhone 15 models, which could definitely help them make it to our best phones list this year. But when compared to the older iPhone 14 Pro, the iPhone 15 lacks a few things, such as a dedicated telephoto camera and a higher display refresh rate. So, should you consider the new iPhone 15 or stick with the older iPhone 14 Pro?

iPhone 15 vs iPhone 14 Pro: Price, availability, and specs

The iPhone 15 was announced on Sept. 12 and is now available as of Sept. 22. It comes in three storage variants and in Blue, Pink, Yellow, Green, and Black. The prices are $799 (128GB), $899 (256GB), and $1,099 (512GB). Cases for iPhone 15 are also on sale.

The iPhone 14 Pro is not listed on the official Apple website (only the iPhone 14 and 14 Plus are), but it’s still available new at popular retailers such as Best Buy. On Best Buy, the iPhone 14 Pro sells at $899 (128GB), $999 (256GB), $1,199 (512GB), and $1,399 (1TB). In terms of color options, you have Space Black, Deep Purple, Silver, and Gold. It's also available renewed at Amazon.


  		• iPhone 15Apple iPhone 14 Pro
    SoCApple A16 BionicApple A16 Bionic
    Display6.1-inch OLED Super Retina XDR6.1-inch OLED, 120Hz, HDR 10
    Storage128GB, 256GB, 512GB128GB, 256GB, 512GB, 1TB
    PortsUSB-CLightning
    Operating SystemiOS 17iOS 16
    Front camera12MP TrueDepth camera12MP, TrueDepth, AF
    Dimensions5.81x2.82x0.31 inches (147.6x71.6x7.8mm)5.81x2.81x0.31 inches (147.5x71.5x7.85mm)
    ColorsBlue, pink, yellow, green, blackSpace Black, Silver, Gold, Deep Purple
    Weight6.02 ounces (171 g)7.27 ounces (206g)
    IP RatingIP68IP68

Design and display

Both the iPhone 15 and iPhone 14 Pro have nearly identical dimensions. The iPhone 15 is 0.1mm thinner, but when seen from the front, the phones are indistinguishable. However, when you hold them, you will notice that the iPhone 14 Pro is slightly heavier (7.27 ounces vs 6.02 ounces), which can be attributed to the build materials and the 14 Pro's extra camera. The iPhone 15 comes with an aluminum frame with a contoured edge design as compared to the iPhone 14 Pro’s stainless steel frame. Turn it around, and that’s when the triple camera setup of the iPhone 14 Pro stands out as compared to the dual camera module on the iPhone 15. Both phones also come with IP68 dust and water resistance.

When it comes to display, both the phones sport a 6.1-inch Super Retina XDR OLED display with a 2556x1179 resolution, both have Dynamic Island, and both displays have an outdoor peak brightness of 2,000 nits. But two major differentiators give the iPhone 14 Pro display an immediate advantage: a 120Hz adaptive refresh rate and an always-on display. Having a higher refresh rate means transitions on the iPhone 14 Pro will be noticeably smoother than the 60Hz refresh rate-sporting iPhone 15. Having an adaptive refresh rate also ensures optimal use of the battery.

Performance and battery

Both phones come with the A16 Bionic chip that houses a 6-core CPU, 5-core GPU, and 16-core Neural Engines. In terms of performance, it’s safe to assume that both phones will be similar. While the iPhone 15 sports a USB Type-C port, the data transfer speeds are limited to USB 2.0 speeds, which are similar to the iPhone 14 Pro’s Lightning Port’s speeds. But only real-life testing can confirm if the iPhone 15 has an edge here.

While both phones come with 6GB RAM, in terms of storage, the iPhone 14 Pro can go up to 1TB. The rated battery life for video playback and video streaming is higher on the iPhone 14 Pro as compared to the iPhone 15, according to the Apple website. We will have to do a side-by-side comparison to see if this holds true.

Cameras

The camera department is where the Pro variants of the iPhone differ from the regular ones. This is also true when you compare iPhones generationally. The iPhone 14 Pro sports a triple camera setup with a 48MP main camera, 12MP ultrawide, and 12MP telephoto camera, whereas the iPhone 15 only has the 48MP main and 12MP ultrawide camera. The iPhone 15's photo will be brighter by default thanks to a wider f/1.6 aperture on the main camera as compared to the f/1.7 on the iPhone 14 Pro's main camera. The front cameras on both phones are identical with 12MP TrueDepth cameras.

For those who like taking portrait photos but forget to activate the Portrait mode on the iPhone 14 series and older, the iPhone 15 offers a solution. It can now capture all the depth information in a photo, which will let you turn a regular photo into one with a shallow depth of field after the fact. You can even change the focus from one subject to another. Yes, this feature has been around on Android phones for a while now, but it's now on iPhone. This feature will also likely make its way to the iPhone 14 Pro, but we don't have confirmation on that yet.

The major advantage that the iPhone 14 Pro has is the ProRAW and macro mode for still photographs and ProRes for video shooting (on the front and rear cameras). These two options, paired with more advanced image stabilization, give the iPhone 14 Pro an edge, especially if you’re someone who’s obsessed with posting photos and videos on social media. Also, thanks to an extra lens, you get a lot more versatility when it comes to the focal length range as compared to the iPhone 15.

iPhone 15 vs iPhone 14 Pro: Which phone is better?

It’s a tough call as both phones are extremely similar. But between the two, I'd still say the iPhone 14 Pro has an edge, as it offers a lot more value for your money. You get a triple-camera setup with some excellent video modes, and the ProMotion 120Hz display is far ahead of the 60Hz display on the iPhone 15. Even if you are considering upgrading from the iPhone X/11 Pro to these phones, I’d recommend the iPhone 14 Pro over the iPhone 15.

Source: Apple
Apple iPhone 14 Pro
Editor's choice

Even though the iPhone 14 Pro is a generation older now, it still offers enough punch and better value for money than the newly announced iPhone 15. The triple cameras, 120 Hz ProMotion display, and better battery life are good enough reasons to opt for the iPhone 14 Pro over the iPhone 15.

$899 at Best Buy $879 at Amazon

The iPhone 15 with its Dynamic Island, special portrait mode, and a USB Type-C port, has defined the path ahead for future non-Pro iPhones. When compared to the iPhone 14 Pro, the iPhone 15 does fall short in the areas mentioned above. It’s not a bad phone, but at the same time, there is nothing compelling to recommend it over the iPhone 14 Pro. If you are already using the iPhone 14 Pro, there is no reason to upgrade to the iPhone 15. Instead, upgrade to the iPhone 15 Pro if your budget allows.

iPhone 15
Runner-up

The only major upgrades with the iPhone 15 are the presence of a Dynamic Island and a USB Type C port. But these aren't compelling reasons to upgrade when the other choice is iPhone 14 Pro.

$730 at Best Buy $799 at Apple $830 at Verizon

Both phones also come with IP68 dust and water resistance.

When it comes to display, both the phones sport a 6.1-inch Super Retina XDR OLED display with a 2556x1179 resolution, both have Dynamic Island, and both displays have an outdoor peak brightness of 2,000 nits. But two major differentiators give the iPhone 14 Pro display an immediate advantage: a 120Hz adaptive refresh rate and an always-on display. Having a higher refresh rate means transitions on the iPhone 14 Pro will be noticeably smoother than the 60Hz refresh rate-sporting iPhone 15. Having an adaptive refresh rate also ensures optimal use of the battery.

Performance and battery

Both phones come with the A16 Bionic chip that houses a 6-core CPU, 5-core GPU, and 16-core Neural Engines. In terms of performance, it’s safe to assume that both phones will be similar. While the iPhone 15 sports a USB Type-C port, the data transfer speeds are limited to USB 2.0 speeds, which are similar to the iPhone 14 Pro’s Lightning Port’s speeds. But only real-life testing can confirm if the iPhone 15 has an edge here.

While both phones come with 6GB RAM, in terms of storage, the iPhone 14 Pro can go up to 1TB. The rated battery life for video playback and video streaming is higher on the iPhone 14 Pro as compared to the iPhone 15, according to the Apple website. We will have to do a side-by-side comparison to see if this holds true.

Cameras

The camera department is where the Pro variants of the iPhone differ from the regular ones. This is also true when you compare iPhones generationally. The iPhone 14 Pro sports a triple camera setup with a 48MP main camera, 12MP ultrawide, and 12MP telephoto camera, whereas the iPhone 15 only has the 48MP main and 12MP ultrawide camera. The iPhone 15's photo will be brighter by default thanks to a wider f/1.6 aperture on the main camera as compared to the f/1.7 on the iPhone 14 Pro's main camera. The front cameras on both phones are identical with 12MP TrueDepth cameras.

For those who like taking portrait photos but forget to activate the Portrait mode on the iPhone 14 series and older, the iPhone 15 offers a solution. It can now capture all the depth information in a photo, which will let you turn a regular photo into one with a shallow depth of field after the fact. You can even change the focus from one subject to another. Yes, this feature has been around on Android phones for a while now, but it's now on iPhone. This feature will also likely make its way to the iPhone 14 Pro, but we don't have confirmation on that yet.

The major advantage that the iPhone 14 Pro has is the ProRAW and macro mode for still photographs and ProRes for video shooting (on the front and rear cameras). These two options, paired with more advanced image stabilization, give the iPhone 14 Pro an edge, especially if you’re someone who’s obsessed with posting photos and videos on social media. Also, thanks to an extra lens, you get a lot more versatility when it comes to the focal length range as compared to the iPhone 15.

iPhone 15 vs iPhone 14 Pro: Which phone is better?

It’s a tough call as both phones are extremely similar. But between the two, I'd still say the iPhone 14 Pro has an edge, as it offers a lot more value for your money. You get a triple-camera setup with some excellent video modes, and the ProMotion 120Hz display is far ahead of the 60Hz display on the iPhone 15. Even if you are considering upgrading from the iPhone X/11 Pro to these phones, I’d recommend the iPhone 14 Pro over the iPhone 15.

Source: Apple
Apple iPhone 14 Pro
Editor's choice

Even though the iPhone 14 Pro is a generation older now, it still offers enough punch and better value for money than the newly announced iPhone 15. The triple cameras, 120 Hz ProMotion display, and better battery life are good enough reasons to opt for the iPhone 14 Pro over the iPhone 15.

$899 at Best Buy $879 at Amazon

The iPhone 15 with its Dynamic Island, special portrait mode, and a USB Type-C port, has defined the path ahead for future non-Pro iPhones. When compared to the iPhone 14 Pro, the iPhone 15 does fall short in the areas mentioned above. It’s not a bad phone, but at the same time, there is nothing compelling to recommend it over the iPhone 14 Pro. If you are already using the iPhone 14 Pro, there is no reason to upgrade to the iPhone 15. Instead, upgrade to the iPhone 15 Pro if your budget allows.

iPhone 15
Runner-up

The only major upgrades with the iPhone 15 are the presence of a Dynamic Island and a USB Type C port. But these aren't compelling reasons to upgrade when the other choice is iPhone 14 Pro.

$730 at Best Buy $799 at Apple $830 at Verizon

item.title: Best cases for HP Spectre Fold
item.itunes:title: Best cases for HP Spectre Fold
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The HP Spectre Fold is HP's flagship foldable laptop, possessing both the power and specifications of a laptop but with the lightweight versatility of a tablet. It features a gorgeous 17-inch OLED display that can fold in half, allowing you to use it in various modes, such as book, tent, or tablet. Whether you use the HP Spectre Fold to work or create, it's a device that can adapt to your specific needs and preferences.

It comes at no short cost though, with a price tag of $5,000. If you want to make the most of this innovative and premium-priced foldable, you'll want a case that can protect it from scratches, drops, and spills. We've done the research for you, and here are our top picks for the best HP Spectre Fold cases. We haven't had the chance to review the HP Spectre Fold yet, but it looks like it could be both one of the best laptops from HP, and one of the best tablets on the market
